summary:
The Bosnian War (1992–1995) was the most destructive conflict in Europe since World War II, claiming approximately 100,000 lives and displacing over two million people in the former Yugoslav republic of Bosnia and Herzegovina. The war began when Bosnian Serb forces, supported by the Yugoslav People's Army and Serbia under Slobodan Milošević, responded to Bosnia's declaration of independence by attacking non-Serb populations and seizing territory in a systematic campaign of ethnic cleansing. Bosniak (Bosnian Muslim) and Croat populations were the primary targets of the Serb campaign, while all sides engaged in atrocities.

Bosnian Serb forces besieged Sarajevo for 44 months — the longest siege of a capital city in the history of modern warfare — subjecting civilians to sniper fire and artillery bombardment. The [[event:2723|Srebrenica Massacre]] of July 1995, in which approximately 8,000 Bosniak men and boys were systematically murdered by Bosnian Serb forces despite the presence of UN peacekeepers, constituted the worst act of genocide in Europe since the Holocaust and galvanized international opinion toward more decisive action.

NATO air strikes against Bosnian Serb positions in the late summer of 1995, combined with a Croatian and Bosniak ground offensive, changed the military balance and brought the parties to the negotiating table. The [[event:3473|Dayton Agreement]] of December 1995 ended the war but left Bosnia with a deeply divided and dysfunctional governance structure. The International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia subsequently prosecuted numerous leaders for war crimes, including the conviction of Radovan Karadžić and Ratko Mladić for genocide.
